# Optional checks
d %>% count(.data[[site_col]], .data[[period_col]], sort = TRUE)
d_glove_doff %>% count(.data[[site_col]], .data[[period_col]], sort = TRUE)

#------------------- Example beta substitution for one PAH -------------------
# Beta-substitution for a single PAH
# Reference: Ganser & Hewett - beta is estimated from detected observations

beta_substitute_pah <- function(data, pah_col_idx, lod_col_idx, pah_label = NULL) {
  
  pah_vals <- data[[pah_col_idx]]
  lod_vals <- data[[lod_col_idx]]
  
  # Classify rows
  has_both   <- !is.na(pah_vals) & !is.na(lod_vals)
  is_detect  <- has_both & pah_vals > lod_vals
  is_nd      <- has_both & pah_vals <= lod_vals
  
  # Detected values only
  x_det <- pah_vals[is_detect]
  l_det <- lod_vals[is_detect]
  
  # Estimate beta: mean ratio of detected value to its paired LOD
  # (a simple moment-based estimate; Ganser & Hewett use MLE of lognormal
  #  truncated at LOD -- this is the approximation form)
  beta_hat <- mean(x_det / l_det)
  
  # Apply substitution: imputed value = beta_hat * LOD for non-detects
  imputed <- ifelse(is_nd, beta_hat * lod_vals, pah_vals)
  
  tibble(
    pah_label   = pah_label,
    pah_raw     = pah_vals,
    lod_val     = lod_vals,
    is_detect   = is_detect,
    is_nd       = is_nd,
    beta_hat    = beta_hat,
    imputed_val = imputed
  )
}

# Run for Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene (pair_id = 9, pah_col = 17, lod_col = 31)
target <- pair_map |> filter(pair_id == 9)

result_dibenzo <- beta_substitute_pah(
  data        = d,
  pah_col_idx = target$pah_col,
  lod_col_idx = target$lod_col,
  pah_label   = target$pah_name
)

result_dibenzo

# Build three-group comparison: Detected (raw), Non-detect (β-imputed), LOD
lod_dat <- result_dibenzo |>
  filter(!is.na(lod_val)) |>
  transmute(
    pah_label = pah_label,
    value     = lod_val,
    group     = "LOD",
    is_detect = FALSE,
    is_nd     = FALSE,
    beta_hat  = NA_real_
  )

# Prepare plot data: detected raw values vs imputed ND values
plot_dat <- result_dibenzo |>
  filter(!is.na(pah_raw) & !is.na(lod_val)) |>
  mutate(
    value = if_else(is_detect, pah_raw, imputed_val),
    group = case_when(
      is_detect ~ "Detected (raw)",
      is_nd     ~ "Non-detect (β-imputed)",
      TRUE      ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(group))

plot_combined <- plot_dat |>
  select(pah_label, value, group, is_detect, is_nd, beta_hat) |>
  bind_rows(lod_dat)

ggplot(plot_combined, aes(x = value, fill = group, color = group)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.30, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_rug(
    data = filter(plot_combined, group != "LOD"),
    alpha = 0.4, length = unit(0.03, "npc")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"         = "#E69F00",
    "Non-detect (β-imputed)" = "#0072B2",
    "LOD"                    = "#CC79A7"
  )) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"         = "#E69F00",
    "Non-detect (β-imputed)" = "#0072B2",
    "LOD"                    = "#CC79A7"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: Detected, β-Imputed, and LOD Distributions",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "β̂ = ", round(unique(plot_dat$beta_hat), 2),
      " | n detected = ", sum(plot_dat$is_detect),
      " | n imputed = ",  sum(plot_dat$is_nd)
    ),
    x     = "Concentration",
    y     = "Density",
    fill  = NULL,
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

#----------------------- Look for LOD clusters -----------------------
result_dibenzo |>
  filter(!is.na(lod_val)) |>
  count(lod_val, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / sum(n))

result_dibenzo |>
  filter(!is.na(lod_val)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lod_val)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, fill = "#CC79A7", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_rug(alpha = 0.4, length = unit(0.03, "npc")) +
  labs(
    title = "LOD Distribution: Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene",
    x     = "LOD Value",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

#----------------------- Implement the MLE approach for beta estimation -----------------------

library(EnvStats)

# --- 1. Extract observed values and censoring indicator from result_dibenzo ---
# Rows with both PAH and LOD values present
mle_dat <- result_dibenzo |>
  filter(!is.na(pah_raw) & !is.na(lod_val))

obs_vals_real <- mle_dat$pah_raw   # detected = true value; ND = LOD value (as recorded)
is_nd_real    <- mle_dat$is_nd     # TRUE = left-censored at LOD

cat("n total (non-missing):", nrow(mle_dat), "\n")
cat("n detected:           ", sum(!is_nd_real), "\n")
cat("n non-detect:         ", sum(is_nd_real), "\n")
cat("Empirical censoring:  ", round(100 * mean(is_nd_real), 1), "%\n")

# Check data for issues
mle_dat |>
  filter(is_nd) |>
  count(pah_raw) |>
  arrange(pah_raw)

obs_vals_real <- if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$lod_val, mle_dat$pah_raw)
is_nd_real    <- mle_dat$is_nd

# --- 2. Fit censored lognormal via MLE ---
fit_real <- elnormCensored(
  x        = obs_vals_real,
  censored = is_nd_real,
  method   = "mle"
)

# First diagnostic check
mu_hat_real    <- fit_real$parameters[["meanlog"]]
sigma_hat_real <- fit_real$parameters[["sdlog"]]

cat("MLE mu_hat:", round(mu_hat_real, 3), "| sigma_hat:", round(sigma_hat_real, 3), "\n")

# --- 3. Derive MLE beta using median LOD as representative censoring point ---
# Beta = E[X | X <= lod_ref] / lod_ref  (truncated lognormal expectation)
lod_ref_real <- median(mle_dat$lod_val)

z_real        <- (log(lod_ref_real) - mu_hat_real) / sigma_hat_real
beta_hat_mle  <- exp(mu_hat_real + sigma_hat_real^2 / 2) *
                   pnorm(z_real - sigma_hat_real) / pnorm(z_real) / lod_ref_real

cat("MLE beta_hat:", round(beta_hat_mle, 3), "\n")
cat("Naive beta_hat (from result_dibenzo):", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3), "\n")

# --- 4. Apply MLE beta substitution ---
mle_dat <- mle_dat |>
  mutate(
    imputed_mle = if_else(is_nd, beta_hat_mle * lod_val, pah_raw)
  )

# --- 5. Compare naive vs. MLE imputation side-by-side ---
# Summary statistics: GM, GSD, P95 for detected-only, naive-imputed, MLE-imputed
gm_fn  <- function(x) exp(mean(log(x[x > 0]), na.rm = TRUE))
gsd_fn <- function(x) exp(sd(log(x[x > 0]),   na.rm = TRUE))

compare_metrics <- tribble(
  ~dataset,          ~values,
  "Detected only",   mle_dat$pah_raw[!is_nd_real],
  "Naive β-imputed", if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$imputed_val, mle_dat$pah_raw),
  "MLE β-imputed",   mle_dat$imputed_mle
) |>
  mutate(
    GM  = map_dbl(values, gm_fn),
    GSD = map_dbl(values, gsd_fn),
    P95 = map_dbl(values, ~quantile(.x, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)),
    n   = map_int(values, ~sum(!is.na(.x)))
  ) |>
  select(-values)

compare_metrics

# --- 6. KDE overlay: Detected, Naive β, MLE β ---
kde_plot_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |>
    transmute(value = pah_raw,     distribution = "Detected (raw)"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = imputed_mle,  distribution = "MLE β-imputed")
)

ggplot(kde_plot_dat, aes(x = value, fill = distribution, color = distribution)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.30, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: Naive vs. MLE β-Substitution",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Naive β̂ = ", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3),
      " | MLE β̂ = ", round(beta_hat_mle, 3),
      " | Censoring = ", round(100 * mean(is_nd_real), 1), "%",
      " | n = ", nrow(mle_dat)
    ),
    x = "Concentration", y = "Density",
    fill = NULL, color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

#---------------- ECDF plots----------------
ecdf_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = imputed_mle,                           distribution = "MLE β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |> transmute(value = pah_raw,            distribution = "Detected (raw)")
)

ggplot(ecdf_dat, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +
  stat_ecdf(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: ECDF Comparison",
    x = "Concentration", y = "Cumulative Probability",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

#----------------------- ECDF plots with the as-is line
ecdf_dat2 <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = pah_raw,                                          distribution = "Raw data (incl. zeros)"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw),             distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = imputed_mle,                                      distribution = "MLE β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |> transmute(value = pah_raw,                        distribution = "Detected (raw)")
)

ggplot(ecdf_dat2, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +
  stat_ecdf(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Raw data (incl. zeros)" = "#CC79A7",
    "Detected (raw)"         = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"        = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"          = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: ECDF Comparison",
    subtitle = paste0("n = ", nrow(mle_dat), " | ", sum(mle_dat$is_nd), " non-detects (", round(100 * mean(mle_dat$is_nd), 1), "%) recorded as zero"),
    x = "Concentration", y = "Cumulative Probability",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

You are an expert R programming assistant with a specialization in biostatistics and toxicology. Your primary user is an experienced research statistician at NIOSH. Your mission is to act as a senior research collaborator, facilitating high-quality, efficient, and reproducible data analysis in the R programming language.

### Core Functions: What This Agent Accomplishes

*   **Code Generation:** Writes clean, efficient, and well-commented R code, with a strong preference for the `tidyverse` ecosystem (`dplyr`, `ggplot2`, `purrr`).
*   **Statistical Modeling:** Provides expert assistance in regression modeling, including linear (`lm`), generalized linear (`glm`), non-linear (`nls`), and mixed-effects (`lme4`, `nlme`) models. Specializes in dose-response analysis and benchmark dose (BMD) modeling.
*   **Data Visualization:** Creates publication-quality data visualizations and model diagnostic plots using `ggplot2`.
*   **Code Optimization & Review:** Refactors existing R code for improved performance, readability, and adherence to best practices.

### Usage Guidelines: When to Use This Agent

*   Use this agent when analyzing rodent toxicological and physicochemical data related to nanoscale particle exposure.
*   Engage this agent for tasks involving quantitative risk assessment, particularly dose-response and BMD analysis.
*   Consult this agent when you need to write or refactor code using `tidyverse` syntax and principles.
*   Ask this agent for recommendations on R packages or statistical methods suitable for your research questions.

### Boundaries: Edges It Won't Cross

### Interaction Protocol

*   **Expected Outputs:**

### Communication & Help

*   This agent will ask clarifying questions if a request is ambiguous to ensure the generated code meets the analytical need (e.g., "Your data seems to have repeated measures. Should I include a random effect for the subject ID?").
*   It will state any assumptions made during code generation (e.g., "Assuming a log-normal distribution for the response variable...").
*   It communicates in a professional, collegial tone, befitting a collaboration between experts.

library(EnvStats)

# --- 1. Extract observed values and censoring indicator from result_dibenzo ---
# Rows with both PAH and LOD values present
mle_dat <- result_dibenzo |>
  filter(!is.na(pah_raw) & !is.na(lod_val))

obs_vals_real <- mle_dat$pah_raw   # detected = true value; ND = LOD value (as recorded)

executions.input:
mle_dat |>
  filter(is_nd) |>
  count(pah_raw) |>
  arrange(pah_raw)

obs_vals_real <- if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$lod_val, mle_dat$pah_raw)
is_nd_real    <- mle_dat$is_nd

# --- 2. Fit censored lognormal via MLE ---
fit_real <- elnormCensored(
  x        = obs_vals_real,
  censored = is_nd_real,
  method   = "mle"
)

# First diagnostic check
mu_hat_real    <- fit_real$parameters[["meanlog"]]
sigma_hat_real <- fit_real$parameters[["sdlog"]]
cat("MLE mu_hat:", round(mu_hat_real, 3), "| sigma_hat:", round(sigma_hat_real, 3), "\n")

# --- 3. Derive MLE beta using median LOD as representative censoring point ---
# Beta = E[X | X <= lod_ref] / lod_ref  (truncated lognormal expectation)
lod_ref_real <- median(mle_dat$lod_val)

z_real        <- (log(lod_ref_real) - mu_hat_real) / sigma_hat_real
beta_hat_mle  <- exp(mu_hat_real + sigma_hat_real^2 / 2) *
                   pnorm(z_real - sigma_hat_real) / pnorm(z_real) / lod_ref_real

cat("MLE beta_hat:", round(beta_hat_mle, 3), "\n")
cat("Naive beta_hat (from result_dibenzo):", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3), "\n")

# --- 4. Apply MLE beta substitution ---
mle_dat <- mle_dat |>
  mutate(
    imputed_mle = if_else(is_nd, beta_hat_mle * lod_val, pah_raw)
  )

# --- 5. Compare naive vs. MLE imputation side-by-side ---
# Summary statistics: GM, GSD, P95 for detected-only, naive-imputed, MLE-imputed
gm_fn  <- function(x) exp(mean(log(x[x > 0]), na.rm = TRUE))
gsd_fn <- function(x) exp(sd(log(x[x > 0]),   na.rm = TRUE))

compare_metrics <- tribble(
  ~dataset,          ~values,
  "Detected only",   mle_dat$pah_raw[!is_nd_real],
  "Naive β-imputed", if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$imputed_val, mle_dat$pah_raw),
  "MLE β-imputed",   mle_dat$imputed_mle
) |>
  mutate(
    GM  = map_dbl(values, gm_fn),
    GSD = map_dbl(values, gsd_fn),
    P95 = map_dbl(values, ~quantile(.x, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)),
    n   = map_int(values, ~sum(!is.na(.x)))
  ) |>
  select(-values)

compare_metrics

# --- 6. KDE overlay: Detected, Naive β, MLE β ---
kde_plot_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |>
    transmute(value = pah_raw,     distribution = "Detected (raw)"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = imputed_mle,  distribution = "MLE β-imputed")
)

ggplot(kde_plot_dat, aes(x = value, fill = distribution, color = distribution)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.30, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: Naive vs. MLE β-Substitution",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Naive β̂ = ", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3),
      " | MLE β̂ = ", round(beta_hat_mle, 3),
      " | Censoring = ", round(100 * mean(is_nd_real), 1), "%",
      " | n = ", nrow(mle_dat)
    ),
    x = "Concentration", y = "Density",
    fill = NULL, color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

ecdf_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = imputed_mle,                           distribution = "MLE β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |> transmute(value = pah_raw,            distribution = "Detected (raw)")
)

ggplot(ecdf_dat, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +
  stat_ecdf(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: ECDF Comparison",
    x = "Concentration", y = "Cumulative Probability",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

ecdf_dat2 <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = pah_raw,                                          distribution = "Raw data (incl. zeros)"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw),             distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = imputed_mle,                                      distribution = "MLE β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |> transmute(value = pah_raw,                        distribution = "Detected (raw)")
)

ggplot(ecdf_dat2, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +

  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Raw data (incl. zeros)" = "#CC79A7",
    "Detected (raw)"         = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"        = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"          = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: ECDF Comparison",
    subtitle = paste0("n = ", nrow(mle_dat), " | ", sum(mle_dat$is_nd), " non-detects (", round(100 * mean(mle_dat$is_nd), 1), "%) recorded as zero"),
    x = "Concentration", y = "Cumulative Probability",

  ) +

response.value:
Sure. Here's the chain of logic step by step:

---

### Goal
Estimate a β such that for any ND observation, its imputed value = β × LOD. We want β derived from the *fitted distribution* rather than the naive lookup table.

---

### Step 1: Fit a censored lognormal via MLE
`elnormCensored()` estimates `μ` (meanlog) and `σ` (sdlog) of the underlying lognormal distribution, using both the 48 detects *and* the information that 400 observations fell below their respective LODs. This gives:

- `mu_hat_real = -2.523`
- `sigma_hat_real = 1.488`

---

### Step 2: Define a reference LOD
Since each observation has its own LOD (82 unique values), we need a single representative censoring point. We use `median(lod_val)` = `lod_ref_real`.

---

### Step 3: Compute the truncated mean
β is defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{E[X \mid X \leq L]}{L}$$

where $L$ is the reference LOD. This is the expected value of the distribution *below the detection limit*, divided by $L$ — giving you the fraction of LOD to use as a substitute.

For a lognormal, this truncated expectation has a closed form:

$$E[X \mid X \leq L] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2} \cdot \frac{\Phi\left(\frac{\ln L - \mu}{\sigma} - \sigma\right)}{\Phi\left(\frac{\ln L - \mu}{\sigma}\right)}$$

In code that's:

```r
z_real       <- (log(lod_ref_real) - mu_hat_real) / sigma_hat_real
beta_hat_mle <- exp(mu_hat_real + sigma_hat_real^2 / 2) *
                  pnorm(z_real - sigma_hat_real) / pnorm(z_real) / lod_ref_real
```

The `pnorm(z_real)` in the denominator is $P(X \leq L)$ — the normalizing constant that conditions the expectation on being below the LOD. Dividing by `lod_ref_real` at the end converts the truncated mean into a *fraction of LOD*, which is β.

---

### Why β̂_MLE = 0.207 is so low
With `mu_hat_real = -2.523`, the MLE-fitted distribution is centered well below the LOD. The 89% censoring is pulling `μ` sharply downward because the optimizer "sees" 400 observations piling up below the LOD and concludes the true distribution must sit mostly beneath it. The resulting β is small because the truncated mean is a small fraction of the LOD.

The naive β̂ = 2.385 has no such constraint — it's a fixed lookup derived from a theoretical distribution assumption, not conditioned on your actual censoring structure.

Join polars DataFrames to combine related data:

```python
import polars as pl

df_customers.join(df_orders, on="customer_id", how="left")
```

You can apply numpy functions to compute numerical operations:

```python
import numpy as np
import polars as pl

df.with_columns(
    pl.col("score").map_elements(lambda x: np.log(x), return_dtype=pl.Float64).alias("log_score")
)
```

- Use `.explode()` to unpack list columns into separate rows.
- Use `pl.concat()` for vertical or horizontal concatenation.
- Use `pl.col()` to reference columns in expressions.
- Filter with `.filter()`, select with `.select()`, add columns with `.with_columns()`.

```python
)
```

- Use `from plotnine import *` to import all functions in the global namespace; do this only once per analysis.
- Don't change the theme (don't use a `theme_()` function) unless explicitly requested

executions.input:
mle_dat |>
  filter(is_nd) |>
  count(pah_raw) |>
  arrange(pah_raw)

obs_vals_real <- if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$lod_val, mle_dat$pah_raw)
is_nd_real    <- mle_dat$is_nd

# --- 2. Fit censored lognormal via MLE ---
fit_real <- elnormCensored(
  x        = obs_vals_real,
  censored = is_nd_real,
  method   = "mle"
)

# First diagnostic check
mu_hat_real    <- fit_real$parameters[["meanlog"]]
sigma_hat_real <- fit_real$parameters[["sdlog"]]
cat("MLE mu_hat:", round(mu_hat_real, 3), "| sigma_hat:", round(sigma_hat_real, 3), "\n")

# --- 3. Derive MLE beta using median LOD as representative censoring point ---
# Beta = E[X | X <= lod_ref] / lod_ref  (truncated lognormal expectation)
lod_ref_real <- median(mle_dat$lod_val)

z_real        <- (log(lod_ref_real) - mu_hat_real) / sigma_hat_real
beta_hat_mle  <- exp(mu_hat_real + sigma_hat_real^2 / 2) *
                   pnorm(z_real - sigma_hat_real) / pnorm(z_real) / lod_ref_real

cat("MLE beta_hat:", round(beta_hat_mle, 3), "\n")
cat("Naive beta_hat (from result_dibenzo):", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3), "\n")

# --- 4. Apply MLE beta substitution ---
mle_dat <- mle_dat |>
  mutate(
    imputed_mle = if_else(is_nd, beta_hat_mle * lod_val, pah_raw)
  )

# --- 5. Compare naive vs. MLE imputation side-by-side ---
# Summary statistics: GM, GSD, P95 for detected-only, naive-imputed, MLE-imputed
gm_fn  <- function(x) exp(mean(log(x[x > 0]), na.rm = TRUE))
gsd_fn <- function(x) exp(sd(log(x[x > 0]),   na.rm = TRUE))

compare_metrics <- tribble(
  ~dataset,          ~values,
  "Detected only",   mle_dat$pah_raw[!is_nd_real],
  "Naive β-imputed", if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$imputed_val, mle_dat$pah_raw),
  "MLE β-imputed",   mle_dat$imputed_mle
) |>
  mutate(
    GM  = map_dbl(values, gm_fn),
    GSD = map_dbl(values, gsd_fn),
    P95 = map_dbl(values, ~quantile(.x, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)),
    n   = map_int(values, ~sum(!is.na(.x)))
  ) |>
  select(-values)

compare_metrics

# --- 6. KDE overlay: Detected, Naive β, MLE β ---
kde_plot_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |>
    transmute(value = pah_raw,     distribution = "Detected (raw)"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = imputed_mle,  distribution = "MLE β-imputed")
)

ggplot(kde_plot_dat, aes(x = value, fill = distribution, color = distribution)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.30, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: Naive vs. MLE β-Substitution",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Naive β̂ = ", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3),
      " | MLE β̂ = ", round(beta_hat_mle, 3),
      " | Censoring = ", round(100 * mean(is_nd_real), 1), "%",
      " | n = ", nrow(mle_dat)
    ),
    x = "Concentration", y = "Density",
    fill = NULL, color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

ecdf_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = imputed_mle,                           distribution = "MLE β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |> transmute(value = pah_raw,            distribution = "Detected (raw)")
)

ggplot(ecdf_dat, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +
  stat_ecdf(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: ECDF Comparison",
    x = "Concentration", y = "Cumulative Probability",

  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

ecdf_dat2 <- bind_rows(

  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw),             distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),

)

ggplot(ecdf_dat2, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +

response.value: Let me pull up the paper to give you a precise answer.

The short answer is: **the MLE beta approach we implemented is not from Ganser & Hewett**. Here's the distinction:

---

### What Ganser & Hewett actually describe

Their β-substitution is a **moment-based, closed-form algorithm** derived from the *uncensored observations only*. The steps are:

1. Compute ȳ (mean of log-transformed detects) and ŝ_y (SD of log-transformed detects)
2. Compute a Z-score corresponding to the censoring fraction k/n
3. Plug those into closed-form expressions involving `pnorm()` and `dnorm()` to get β_GM and β_MEAN

**This is the "naive" approach** already implemented in your script — it uses only the detected observations and the censoring fraction, with no MLE fitting of the full censored distribution.

---

### What we implemented as "MLE beta"

Our MLE approach fits a censored lognormal to **all 448 observations** via `elnormCensored()`, extracts `μ` and `σ`, then derives β from the truncated lognormal expectation formula. This is a **custom extension** — not something Ganser & Hewett describe or validate.

---

### Practical implication

If you're positioning this analysis against the prior Wilkinson et al. study, the Ganser & Hewett naive β is the defensible, paper-validated method. Our MLE β was exploratory — useful for diagnosing instability at 89% censoring, but not something you'd cite G&H to justify.

response.value: You're right — Ganser & Hewett do describe an MLE method. Let me look at the summary more carefully.

You're right that G&H's "MLE METHOD" section assumes a lognormal distribution — but it's important to understand *what* they use MLE for vs. what the β-substitution does. Here's the distinction:

---

### G&H's MLE Method
This is the **benchmark comparator**, not the β-substitution itself. It:
1. Assumes lognormality
2. Uses Newton-Raphson to maximize a **full likelihood** that accounts for both detected observations (exact values) and censored observations (probability mass below LOD)
3. Directly yields `μ` (meanlog) and `σ` (sdlog) as the fitted parameters

This is essentially what `elnormCensored()` does.

---

### G&H's β-Substitution Method
This is their innovation — and critically, it does **not** use MLE at all. Instead:
1. It uses only the **detected observations** to compute ȳ and ŝ_y (mean and SD of log-detects)
2. It uses the **censoring fraction** k/n to find a Z-score corresponding to the detection threshold
3. It plugs those into closed-form `pnorm()`/`dnorm()` expressions to derive β

The key insight is that β is constructed so that substituting β × LOD for each ND and then computing ordinary sample statistics yields estimates that **approximate what MLE would give** — without actually running MLE. That's why the paper shows β-substitution tracking MLE so closely across their simulation conditions.

---

### Bottom Line
G&H use MLE as the gold standard to *validate against*, not as an input to β. Their β comes entirely from the detects + censoring fraction. What we implemented as "MLE β" is a custom hybrid that G&H never describe — which is why the two betas (0.207 vs. 2.385) diverge so dramatically at 89% censoring.

parts.text: Okay thanks. So to clarify the arguments here, it makes sense to begin following the process (i.e., beta substitution) from the Wilkinson paper. We have explored that option for one PAH, Dibenzo, which has a very high censored rate (~90%). However, diagnostics revealed an upward bias - meaning the nondetects were typically estimated to have values ABOVE the detected values. As an alternative, we have explored a maximum likelihood approach. This approach isn't just simply assuming a lognormal distribution of Dibenzo measurements, but a truncated lognormal to incorporate the censoring. The assumption is still that there is a parameter, Beta, that can be used to to impute the censored values by multiplying beta with the LOD (this follows the traditional approach of LOD/2 or sqrt(2)). Another assumption is that the median of the LODs is a "typical" LOD. Is this correct so far?

executions.input:
mle_dat |>
  filter(is_nd) |>
  count(pah_raw) |>
  arrange(pah_raw)

obs_vals_real <- if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$lod_val, mle_dat$pah_raw)
is_nd_real    <- mle_dat$is_nd

# --- 2. Fit censored lognormal via MLE ---
fit_real <- elnormCensored(
  x        = obs_vals_real,
  censored = is_nd_real,
  method   = "mle"
)

# First diagnostic check
mu_hat_real    <- fit_real$parameters[["meanlog"]]
sigma_hat_real <- fit_real$parameters[["sdlog"]]
cat("MLE mu_hat:", round(mu_hat_real, 3), "| sigma_hat:", round(sigma_hat_real, 3), "\n")

# --- 3. Derive MLE beta using median LOD as representative censoring point ---
# Beta = E[X | X <= lod_ref] / lod_ref  (truncated lognormal expectation)
lod_ref_real <- median(mle_dat$lod_val)

z_real        <- (log(lod_ref_real) - mu_hat_real) / sigma_hat_real
beta_hat_mle  <- exp(mu_hat_real + sigma_hat_real^2 / 2) *
                   pnorm(z_real - sigma_hat_real) / pnorm(z_real) / lod_ref_real

cat("MLE beta_hat:", round(beta_hat_mle, 3), "\n")
cat("Naive beta_hat (from result_dibenzo):", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3), "\n")

# --- 4. Apply MLE beta substitution ---
mle_dat <- mle_dat |>
  mutate(
    imputed_mle = if_else(is_nd, beta_hat_mle * lod_val, pah_raw)
  )

# --- 5. Compare naive vs. MLE imputation side-by-side ---
# Summary statistics: GM, GSD, P95 for detected-only, naive-imputed, MLE-imputed
gm_fn  <- function(x) exp(mean(log(x[x > 0]), na.rm = TRUE))
gsd_fn <- function(x) exp(sd(log(x[x > 0]),   na.rm = TRUE))

compare_metrics <- tribble(
  ~dataset,          ~values,
  "Detected only",   mle_dat$pah_raw[!is_nd_real],
  "Naive β-imputed", if_else(mle_dat$is_nd, mle_dat$imputed_val, mle_dat$pah_raw),
  "MLE β-imputed",   mle_dat$imputed_mle
) |>
  mutate(
    GM  = map_dbl(values, gm_fn),
    GSD = map_dbl(values, gsd_fn),
    P95 = map_dbl(values, ~quantile(.x, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)),
    n   = map_int(values, ~sum(!is.na(.x)))
  ) |>
  select(-values)

compare_metrics

# --- 6. KDE overlay: Detected, Naive β, MLE β ---
kde_plot_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |>
    transmute(value = pah_raw,     distribution = "Detected (raw)"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |>
    transmute(value = imputed_mle,  distribution = "MLE β-imputed")
)

ggplot(kde_plot_dat, aes(x = value, fill = distribution, color = distribution)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.30, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Detected (raw)"   = "#E69F00",
    "Naive β-imputed"  = "#0072B2",
    "MLE β-imputed"    = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Dibenzo(a,h)anthracene: Naive vs. MLE β-Substitution",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Naive β̂ = ", round(unique(result_dibenzo$beta_hat[!is.na(result_dibenzo$beta_hat)]), 3),
      " | MLE β̂ = ", round(beta_hat_mle, 3),
      " | Censoring = ", round(100 * mean(is_nd_real), 1), "%",
      " | n = ", nrow(mle_dat)
    ),
    x = "Concentration", y = "Density",
    fill = NULL, color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

ecdf_dat <- bind_rows(
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = if_else(is_nd, imputed_val, pah_raw), distribution = "Naive β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> transmute(value = imputed_mle,                           distribution = "MLE β-imputed"),
  mle_dat |> filter(!is_nd) |> transmute(value = pah_raw,            distribution = "Detected (raw)")
)

ggplot(ecdf_dat, aes(x = value, color = distribution)) +

response.value:
Yes, that's a correct summary. Let me just sharpen one point:

---

### What's right
- The MLE fits a **censored lognormal** (not just a lognormal) — the likelihood explicitly accounts for the fact that 400 observations are only known to fall *below* their respective LODs
- The goal is still to produce a single scalar β, consistent with the G&H framework, so that imputation = β × LOD
- Using `median(lod_val)` as a representative censoring point is an **auxiliary assumption** needed to collapse the 82 unique LODs into a single reference point for the β calculation

---

### One nuance worth flagging

The **truncated lognormal** framing is slightly more precise than "censored lognormal." Specifically:

- The **fitting step** (`elnormCensored`) uses a censored likelihood — it models the full distribution and treats NDs as contributing $P(X \leq LOD_i)$ to the likelihood
- The **β derivation step** then computes $E[X \mid X \leq L_{ref}]$ — this is where the truncated distribution comes in, conditioning the expectation on being below the median LOD

So the censored likelihood is used to estimate μ and σ, and then a truncated expectation is used to convert those parameters into β. They're related but distinct steps.

---

### The core tension
The median LOD assumption is doing real work here. With 82 unique LODs spanning a range, "what is a typical LOD?" is not trivial — and the β you get is sensitive to that choice. This is arguably the weakest assumption in the MLE approach, and worth being explicit about if you write this up.

```python
import polars as pl

df.with_columns(
    pl.col("score").map_elements(lambda x: np.log(x), return_dtype=pl.Float64).alias("log_score")
)
```

- Use `.explode()` to unpack list columns into separate rows.
- Use `pl.concat()` for vertical or horizontal concatenation.
- Use `pl.col()` to reference columns in expressions.
- Filter with `.filter()`, select with `.select()`, add columns with `.with_columns()`.
- Handle nulls with `.fill_null()` and cast types with `.cast()`.
- Create polars Series from numpy arrays with `pl.Series()`.
</polars>

<plotnine>
**plotnine** uses the grammar of graphics to build layered visualizations:

```python
from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, labs, theme_minimal

(
    ggplot(df, aes(x="x_coord", y="y_coord", color="category"))
    + geom_point(size=3, alpha=0.7)
    + labs(title="Scatter Plot", x="X-coordinate", y="Y-coordinate")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

- Use `from plotnine import *` to import all functions in the global namespace; do this only once per analysis.
- Don't change the theme (don't use a `theme_()` function) unless explicitly requested
- Write each function on a separate line
- Insert a newline between the opening parenthesis and `ggplot`
- The plus operator (`+`) should be at the start of the line
- plotnine accepts Polars DataFrames, so don't use `.to_pandas()`
- To display a plot, just use `ggplot` by itself, or if you save it to a variable, like `p`, just use `p` to display it. Do NOT use `p.show()` or `print(p)`, because they will not display the plot correctly.
</plotnine>

The following examples provide some rules-of-thumb on analyzing data with R.

<examples-r>
When visualizing data with ggplot2, keep your initial plotting code minimal--just a dataset, aesthetic, geometry, and possibly labels.

```r
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Penguin Flipper Length vs. Body Mass",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)",
    color = "Species"
  )
```

* Refrain from applying `geom_smooth()` unless the user requests it.

In dplyr, iterate across columns using `across()`:

```r
diamonds |>
  summarize(
    across(where(is.numeric), list(mean = mean, sd = sd), na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

* Prefer the new `.by` syntax over `group_by()` when applying operations by group.
* Use dplyr's `join_by(col_name)` helper when joining, as in `flights |> left_join(airlines, join_by(carrier))`.

### Agent Mission

You are an expert R programming assistant with a specialization in biostatistics and toxicology. Your primary user is an experienced research statistician at NIOSH. Your mission is to act as a senior research collaborator, facilitating high-quality, efficient, and reproducible data analysis in the R programming language.

### Core Functions: What This Agent Accomplishes

*   **Code Generation:** Writes clean, efficient, and well-commented R code, with a strong preference for the `tidyverse` ecosystem (`dplyr`, `ggplot2`, `purrr`).
*   **Statistical Modeling:** Provides expert assistance in regression modeling, including linear (`lm`), generalized linear (`glm`), non-linear (`nls`), and mixed-effects (`lme4`, `nlme`) models. Specializes in dose-response analysis and benchmark dose (BMD) modeling.
*   **Data Visualization:** Creates publication-quality data visualizations and model diagnostic plots using `ggplot2`.
*   **Code Optimization & Review:** Refactors existing R code for improved performance, readability, and adherence to best practices.

### Usage Guidelines: When to Use This Agent

*   Use this agent when analyzing rodent toxicological and physicochemical data related to nanoscale particle exposure.
*   Engage this agent for tasks involving quantitative risk assessment, particularly dose-response and BMD analysis.
*   Consult this agent when you need to write or refactor code using `tidyverse` syntax and principles.
*   Ask this agent for recommendations on R packages or statistical methods suitable for your research questions.

### Boundaries: Edges It Won't Cross

*   **Scientific Interpretation:** This agent will **not** make definitive scientific interpretations of results. It provides the statistical tools and outputs; the user, as the domain expert, is the final authority on research conclusions.
*   **Language Limitation:** This agent is an R specialist. It will **not** write or debug code in other programming languages (e.g., Python, SAS).
*   **Data Security:** This agent will **not** access, store, or transmit any raw data, sensitive information, Personally Identifiable Information (PII), or Protected Health Information (PHI). All interactions are stateless.

### Interaction Protocol

*   **Ideal Inputs:**
    *   Natural language requests describing a desired analysis or visualization (e.g., "Plot the respiratory rate against particle concentration, faceted by exposure duration").
    *   R code snippets that need to be reviewed, debugged, or optimized.
    *   Specific questions about R functions, package usage, or statistical implementation (e.g., "What is the best way to handle non-convergence in an `nlme` model?").
*   **Expected Outputs:**
    *   Well-commented R code that is immediately usable and easy to understand.
    *   Clear explanations of *why* a certain approach or function was chosen.
    *   Constructive suggestions for improving code quality, statistical rigor, and reproducibility.

### Communication & Help

*   This agent will ask clarifying questions if a request is ambiguous to ensure the generated code meets the analytical need (e.g., "Your data seems to have repeated measures. Should I include a random effect for the subject ID?").
*   It will state any assumptions made during code generation (e.g., "Assuming a log-normal distribution for the response variable...").
*   It communicates in a professional, collegial tone, befitting a collaboration between experts.
